text:
name:	griffithlab/civic-pubtator
description:	Version of tmvar working on ARM with detailed instructions and examples
--
# civic-pubtator

Pipeline for annotating biomedical PDFs with named entities relevant to CIViC curators: genes, variants, drugs, diseases, species, and cell lines.

---

## Acknowledgements

This project is an attempt to create a standalone, reproducible version of the
[PubTator 3.0](https://doi.org/10.1093/nar/gkae235) entity recognition and
normalization pipeline. PubTator 3.0 is developed and maintained by the National
Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI). Because the PubTator 3.0 pipeline is
not publicly portable, this implementation reverse-engineers the component tools
based on their documentation and the PubTator 3.0 publication. See
[workflow_description.md](workflow_description.md) for a detailed description of
the pipeline and how each tool is used.

The following tools are used, roughly in pipeline order:

| Tool | Role |
|---|---|
| [GROBID](https://github.com/kermitt2/grobid) | Converts PDFs to structured BioC XML (title, abstract, body, figures, tables) |
| [AIONER](https://github.com/ncbi/AIONER/) | Deep-learning NER for all six entity types (genes, chemicals, diseases, species, variants, cell lines) |
| [GNorm2](https://github.com/ncbi/GNorm2/) | Gene and species NER + normalization to NCBI Gene / NCBI Taxonomy IDs |
| [tmVar3](https://github.com/ncbi/tmVar3) | Genetic variant NER + normalization to dbSNP RS#, HGVS, and ClinGen CA# |
| [NLMChem](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33767203/) | Chemical/drug normalization to MeSH identifiers (reads AIONER NER output) |
| [TaggerOne](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27283952/) | Disease NER + normalization to MeSH/OMIM identifiers |

---

## Table of contents

1. [Quick start](#quick-start)
2. [Automated monitoring service](#automated-monitoring-service)
3. [Directory structure](#directory-structure)
4. [Running the pipeline](#running-the-pipeline)
   - [Basic usage](#basic-usage)
   - [Supplementary files](#supplementary-files)
   - [All options](#all-options)
5. [Output files](#output-files)

---

## Quick start — Google Cloud

The pipeline is designed to run on Google Cloud Platform (GCP). Large tool model
files (CRF models, BERT weights, SQLite databases) live in a GCS bucket and are
synced to the VM on startup; publication data is synced separately before and
after each run.

### 0. One-time project setup

Run once ever to create the VPC network, subnet, firewall rule, and GCS bucket
that all VMs share:

```bash
bash src/cloud/create_gcp_resources.sh \

188 cp_CLAUDE.md
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Project Goal

**civic-pubtator** aims to reproduce much of the functionality of PubTator 3.0 to annotate biomedical literature with entities important to CIViC curators: **Genes, Variants, Drugs, Diseases, Species, and Cell Lines**.

Because the PubTator 3.0 pipeline is not publicly portable, it is being reverse-engineered and adapted based on the component tools' documentation and the PubTator 3.0 publication. The published pipeline works as follows:

- **NER** — AIONER (deep-learning transformer) recognizes six entity types: genes/proteins, chemicals, diseases, species, genetic variants, cell lines.
- **Normalization** — Specialized mappers link each entity type to a database identifier:
  - Genes/species → NCBI Gene / NCBI Taxonomy via **GNorm2**
  - Genetic variants → dbSNP RS# or HGVS notation via **tmVar3**
  - Chemicals → MeSH via NLM-Chem
  - Diseases / cell lines → MeSH / Cellosaurus via TaggerOne
- **Relation extraction** — BioREx identifies 12 common relation types between entities.

The **current pipeline** approximates this with tools that are available. GROBID output feeds two independent annotation branches:

```
PDFs → GROBID → 02_grobid/ (BioC XML)
                     │
          ┌──────────┼─────────────────────┐
          │          │                     │
       GNorm2     AIONER              TaggerOne
       (gene +    (all 6 NER          (disease NER +
        species    entity types)       normalization)
        norm)         │                     │
          │        NLMChem            07_taggerone/
       tmVar3      (chemical norm;
       (variant +   reads AIONER out)
        gene norm)     │
          │        06_nlmchem/
       04_tmvar3/
          │
     src/pipeline_steps/report_civic_pubtator.py → report_<pmid>.html
```

All tools run in **batch mode** — all documents across all groups (main + supplementary) are processed in a single tool invocation to amortize model-loading startup costs. The orchestrating script is `civic_pubtator.py`.

The `src/` directory is organised into three subdirectories:
- `src/pipeline_steps/` — per-step wrapper scripts invoked by the orchestrator (`pdf_to_bioc.py`, `prepare_supplementary.py`, `run_gnorm2.py`, `run_tmvar.py`, `run_aioner.py`, `run_nlmchem.py`, `run_taggerone.py`, `report_civic_pubtator.py`)
- `src/setup_conda_envs/` — one-time environment setup scripts (`setup_gnorm2_conda.sh`, `setup_aioner_conda.sh`, `setup_nlmchem_conda.sh`, `check_gpu.py`)
- `src/cloud/` and `src/mac/` — infrastructure and platform-specific helpers

Per-publication directory structure:
```
<pub_dir>/01_source/        ← source PDFs (+ s/ subdir for supplementary)
<pub_dir>/02_grobid/        ← GROBID BioC XML output
<pub_dir>/03_gnorm2/        ← GNorm2 annotated BioC XML (gene + species)
<pub_dir>/04_tmvar3/        ← tmVar3 BioC XML + PubTator files (variant + gene)
<pub_dir>/05_aioner/        ← AIONER NER-annotated BioC XML (all 6 entity types)
<pub_dir>/06_nlmchem/       ← NLMChem BioC XML (chemical → MeSH); + abbreviations/
<pub_dir>/07_taggerone/     ← TaggerOne BioC XML (disease → MeSH/OMIM)
<pub_dir>/MANIFEST.txt      ← run metadata
<pub_dir>/pipeline_stats.tsv / pipeline_stats.log
<pub_dir>/report_<pmid>.html
```

The HTML report (`src/src/pipeline_steps/report_civic_pubtator.py`) currently reads from:
- `04_tmvar3/*.PubTator` — passages, variants, genes, species, cell lines
- `06_nlmchem/*.xml` — chemical annotations (merged in, highlighted in fuchsia)

Steps 05 (AIONER) and 07 (TaggerOne) outputs are produced but not yet read by the reporter.

---

## Known Tool Capabilities and Limitations

### TaggerOne (v0.2.1 public release)

- **Disease-only.** The `model_DISE.bin` model was trained with `--entityTypes Disease` on NCBI Disease + BC5CDR corpora. It produces only `Disease` annotations normalized to MeSH/OMIM. Chemicals are explicitly ignored during training.
- **No normalization-only mode.** The PubTator 3.0 paper describes TaggerOne running in a "normalization-only mode" (applying normalization to pre-existing AIONER spans). That mode was developed by NCBI and was **never publicly released**. The v0.2.1 JAR has no equivalent.
- **Passing AIONER output to TaggerOne would not help.** `ProcessText` ignores existing annotations in the input BioC XML and always runs its own NER from scratch.
- **No cell line model.** No Cellosaurus lexicon or cell-line-trained model exists in the public v0.2.1 distribution.

### GNorm2 — CellLine annotations

GNorm2 produces `CellLine` annotations (e.g. NIH3T3, SK-MEL-208, HEK293) that flow through tmVar3 into the PubTator files and report. However, the identifiers assigned are **NCBI Taxonomy IDs** (`9606` = human, `10090` = mouse), not Cellosaurus accessions (CVCL_xxxx). GNorm2 identifies the host organism of the cell line, not the specific cell line entry. Cellosaurus normalization would require a separate tool.

### NLMChem

Reads AIONER output (step 5) as input. Produces `Chemical` annotations normalized to MeSH identifiers. Unresolvable chemicals receive identifier `-`. Now integrated into the HTML report via `parse_bioc_chemicals()` in `src/pipeline_steps/report_civic_pubtator.py`.

---

## What This Project Is (tmVar3 component)

tmVar3 is a Java-based biomedical text-mining pipeline that identifies and normalizes genetic variant mentions (DNA mutations, protein mutations, SNPs) in scientific literature. It extracts variants from PubTator or BioC XML input and maps them to standard identifiers (dbSNP RS#, ClinGen Allele Registry CA#, HGVS notation).

## Running the Tool

```bash
# Standard run — requires 5–10 GB heap
java -Xmx5G -Xms5G -jar tmVar.jar [InputFolder] [OutputFolder]

# Example
java -Xmx5G -Xms5G -jar tmVar.jar input output
```

Optional positional arguments (defaults in parens):
- `TrainTest` — `Test` (default) or `Train`
- `DeleteTmp` — delete temporary CRF files (default `true`)
- `DisplayRSnumOnly` — suppress CA# output (default `false`)
- `HideMultipleResult` — hide ambiguous mappings (default `false`)

The shell wrapper `tmVar.sh` sets these flags.

## Building / Compiling

The pre-built `tmVar.jar` (56 MB) is checked in. To recompile the Java source, use standard `javac` with the JARs in `bin/` and `lib/` on the classpath.

The C++ CRF++ module must be compiled separately (Linux only):

```bash
bash Installation.sh        # runs ./configure && make inside CRF/
```

Pre-compiled binaries (`CRF/crf_test`, `CRF/crf_learn`) are included for Linux.

## Input / Output Formats

**Input** — either format is auto-detected:
- **PubTator** — `PMID|t|Title` / `PMID|a|Abstract` lines, tab-separated annotation lines
- **BioC XML** — structured XML conforming to `BioC.dtd`

**Output** — BioC XML with added annotations containing:
- `tmVar` component breakdown
- HGVS notation
- `VariantGroup`, `CorrespondingGene`, RS#/CA# identifiers

## Architecture Overview

The pipeline runs sequentially through four main classes:

```
BioCConverter  →  MentionRecognition  →  PostProcessing  →  ToHGVs
(parse input)     (CRF feature          (structure          (HGVS + DB
                   extraction &          detected             normalization)
                   inference)            mentions)
```

**`tmVar.java`** — main entry point; loads all static resources (POS model, regex patterns, frequency tables, DB mappings) into global `HashMap`s, then drives the pipeline.

**`MentionRecognition.java`** — tokenizes and POS-tags each sentence (Stanford tagger), generates feature vectors, shells out to `CRF/crf_test` for sequence labeling, returns labeled spans.

**`PostProcessing.java`** — the largest class (259 KB); decomposes CRF output into structured variant mentions, handles sentence-level aggregation, formats BioC/PubTator output.

**`ToHGVs.java`** — converts variant strings to HGVS nomenclature and resolves RS#/CA# via SQLite lookups. Queries the chromosome-sharded `Database/var2rs_g.*.db` files and related databases.

**`CorrespondGene.java`** — optional gene-linking step; requires GNormPlus gene annotations as pre-input. Links variant mentions to specific gene IDs.

**`PrefixTree.java`** — trie-based dictionary for fast gene/species mention lookup with Greek letter and special character normalization.

**`BioCConverter.java`** — parses BioC XML using the Woodstox streaming parser; extracts passages and validates format.

## Key Model / Data Files

| Path | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `CRF/MentionExtractionUB.Model` | CRF model for identifying variant spans (232 MB) |
| `CRF/ComponentExtraction.Model` | CRF model for decomposing variant components (882 MB) |
| `lib/RegEx/DNAMutation.RegEx.txt` | Regex patterns for DNA variants |
| `lib/RegEx/ProteinMutation.RegEx.txt` | Regex patterns for protein variants |
| `lib/RegEx/SNP.RegEx.txt` | Regex patterns for SNP mentions |
| `lib/RegEx/MF.RegEx.2.txt` | Feature patterns for ML (70 KB) |
| `lib/taggers/english-left3words-distsim.tagger` | Stanford POS tagger model |
| `Database/*.db` | SQLite databases for variant normalization (~550 GB total) |
| `bin/filtering.txt` | False-positive filtering rules |

## Java Dependencies (all pre-bundled in `bin/` and `lib/`)

`bioc.jar`, `stanford-postagger.jar`, `mallet.jar`, `mallet-deps.jar`, `org.tartarus.snowball.jar`, `sqlite-jdbc-3.8.11.2.jar`, `pengyifan-pubtator.jar`, `java-json.jar`, `commons-lang-2.4.jar`

## Preprocessing Utilities

```bash
# Extract PubMed articles to PubTator format
perl PreProcessing.pl

# Convert PDF to BioC XML via GROBID (requires running GROBID service)
python3 src/pipeline_steps/pdf_to_bioc.py
```

## Gene Normalization Integration

tmVar3 can optionally link variants to genes using GNormPlus output. Run GNormPlus on the input first so that gene mention annotations are present in the BioC XML; `CorrespondGene.java` then maps variants to the nearest gene in the same sentence/passage. Without GNormPlus input the tool still extracts and normalizes variants but does not produce `CorrespondingGene` fields.

text: name: griffithlab/civic-mcp-server description: -- # CIViC MCP Server MCP-supported Chatbot for CIViC users: https://civicdb.org/mcp-chat Preprint: https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.10.13.682185v1 This is a Cloudflare Workers-based Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that provides tools for querying the CIViC (Clinical Interpretation of Variants in Cancer) API. The CIViC database is a crowd-sourced repository of clinical interpretations of cancer variants. This MCP server enables structured queries and data analysis of cancer genomics information through natural language interactions with AI assistants. Python 3.11.7 ```bash pip install -r requirements.txt ``` ## Directly Querying the MCP Server The MCP Server can be called directly from the command line with the optional arguments disease and therapy to get CIViC evidence items: ```bash python MCP_query_evidence.py --mp "EGFR" --disease "Lung Non-small Cell Carcinoma" --therapy "Erlotinib" ``` ## Locally Hosting The CIViC MCP Server We provide an example of doing this with GPT4o-mini. A personal API key is required, update the variable OPENAI_API_KEY in local_hosting/message_MCP_CIViC.py ```bash python local_hosting/message_MCP_CIViC.py --msg "What is the clinical significance of EGFR variants in CIViC?" ``` ## Adding as a Claude Connector ## Usage The server provides two main tools: 1. **`get_variant_evidence`**: Return up to 50 evidence items for a CIViC molecular profile 2. **`get_variant_assertions`**: Return CIViC assertions for a molecular profile ## Evaluation Scripts All evaluation files are located in eval_QA_experiment. The file agent_mode_run_results.csv contains the agent mode prompts and responses; the associated chat URLs were created but may no longer be available. QA_triplet_dataset.csv contains the 100 unique (Molecular Profile, Disease, Therapy) triplets along with their expected answers. ## License MIT License with Academic Citation Requirement - see text:
name:	griffithlab/bio-text-annotator
description:	
--
# bio-text-annotator

A lightweight, local pipeline for extracting biomedical entities from scientific documents and generating structured reports.

This project processes user-provided documents (e.g., PDFs for a single publication) and extracts biomedical entities such as genetic variants. It is inspired by PubTator-style pipelines but designed to run fully locally and remain modular and extensible.

---

## Current Capabilities

At present, the pipeline supports:

- Variant extraction only
- Powered by TMVar3 (Java CRF-based model)
- Detection of:
  - DNA mutations (e.g., `c.233A>G`)
  - Protein mutations (e.g., `p.Asn78Ser`)
  - Deletions, substitutions, frameshifts, nonsense variants
- Extraction from full-text biomedical PDFs via text parsing + BioC conversion

Additional entity types (genes, diseases, chemicals) are planned but not yet enabled.

---

## Pipeline Overview

The system performs the following steps:

1. Load documents from an input directory  
2. Extract text from PDFs using PyMuPDF  
3. Normalize and clean text for biomedical NLP  
4. Convert text to BioC format (TMVar-compatible)  
5. Run TMVar3 (Java CRF pipeline)  
6. Parse PubTator output into structured entities  
7. Aggregate results into a JSON report  

---

## Installation

### Requirements

Install Python dependencies:

```bash
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

## Java Requirement

TMVar3 requires:

- Java 8+ (recommended Java 11 or 17)
- Sufficient heap memory (default uses `-Xmx5G`)

---

text:
===== README lines 60-260 =====

### 1. Start a VM

```bash
bash src/cloud/start_gcp_vm.sh <instance-name> --project <gcp-project>
```

This creates an `n1-highmem-8` VM (52 GB RAM) with an NVIDIA T4 GPU and a 750 GB
SSD. A startup script (`src/cloud/gcp_server_startup.py`) runs automatically
on first boot and handles everything: installing system packages and Java, cloning
the repo, building GROBID (registered as a systemd service), syncing tool model
files from GCS, compiling CRF++ for tmVar3 and GNorm2, and creating all required
conda environments. Watch startup progress from inside the VM with:

```bash
sudo journalctl -u google-startup-scripts -f
```

### 2. One-time user setup (first login only)

After SSH-ing into the VM for the first time, run:

```bash
python3 src/cloud/user_environment_config.py
```

This fixes directory ownership, configures your git identity, generates an SSH
key and walks you through adding it to GitHub, and installs Claude Code.

### 3. Sync publication data

Copy source PDFs down from GCS (or upload a new paper's `01_source/` directory):

```bash
# Download all papers
bash src/cloud/sync_pub_data.sh --bucket civic-pubtator-pub-data down

# Download one paper
bash src/cloud/sync_pub_data.sh --bucket civic-pubtator-pub-data down 28783719
```

### 4. Run the pipeline

```bash
python3 civic_pubtator.py /data/pub-data/28783719/
```

### 5. Upload results and stop the VM

```bash
# Upload results for one paper
bash src/cloud/sync_pub_data.sh --bucket civic-pubtator-pub-data up 28783719

# Stop the VM to save money (preserves disk; restart with: gcloud compute instances start <instance-name> --zone us-central1-f --project <gcp-project>)
gcloud compute instances stop <instance-name> --zone us-central1-f --project <gcp-project>

# Delete the VM when done to avoid ongoing charges (also frees disk)
gcloud compute instances delete <instance-name> --zone us-central1-f --project <gcp-project>
```

**Ballpark costs (us-central1, on-demand, default config):**

| State | Components | ~Cost/day |
|---|---|---|
| Running | n1-highmem-8 ($0.47/hr) + T4 GPU ($0.35/hr) + 750 GB pd-ssd ($0.17/GB/mo) | ~$24 |
| Stopped | 750 GB pd-ssd only | ~$4 |

Stopping vs. deleting is worthwhile if you plan to resume within ~30 days.

---

## Automated monitoring service

The VM runs a systemd service (`civic-pubtator-monitor`) that continuously polls
the GCS bucket for new publications and processes them automatically. This lets
you upload source PDFs to GCS, turn the VM on, and come back to find completed
annotation reports — without manually running the pipeline.

### How it works

The monitor (`src/automation/monitor_pub_bucket.py`) loops on a 10-minute poll
cycle:

1. **Remediation** — fixes any structural issues in the bucket (stray PDFs or
   `s/` directories that weren't placed inside `01_source/`, stale `.DS_Store`
   files).
2. **Detection** — lists all publication directories in the bucket and identifies
   any that are missing expected output files (report HTML, pipeline stats, etc.).
3. **Processing** — for each unprocessed publication, downloads the source files
   to `/data/pub-data/`, runs `civic-pubtator`, and uploads the results back to
   GCS.
4. **Summary** — regenerates the corpus-wide summary report and, if the results
   repo is configured, commits and pushes the updated summary to
   `git@github.com:griffithlab/civic-pubtator-data.git`.

Publications that fail are recorded in `/data/pub-data/.monitor_failures.json`
and skipped on subsequent cycles. Remove a publication's entry from that file to
allow a retry.

### Setup

The service is installed and enabled automatically by `gcp_server_startup.py`
on first boot — no manual setup is required. The results data repo
(`/data/civic-pubtator-data`) is cloned during first-login user setup via
`user_environment_config.py` (Step 4), which prompts for the clone URL.

### Controlling the service

```bash
# Check current status and recent log lines
sudo systemctl status civic-pubtator-monitor

# Stop the service (e.g. to run the pipeline manually without interference)
sudo systemctl stop civic-pubtator-monitor

# Start it again
sudo systemctl start civic-pubtator-monitor

# Disable auto-start on boot (stays stopped across reboots)
sudo systemctl disable civic-pubtator-monitor

# Re-enable auto-start on boot
sudo systemctl enable civic-pubtator-monitor
```

### Viewing logs

All output from the monitor is written to `/data/pub-data/monitor.log`:

```bash
# Follow live output
tail -f /data/pub-data/monitor.log

# Show the last 100 lines
tail -100 /data/pub-data/monitor.log
```

Per-publication pipeline output (stdout/stderr from each `civic-pubtator` run)
is captured separately in `/data/pub-data/<pubid>/pipeline_stats.log`.

### Failure registry

When a publication fails, the monitor records it so it is not retried
automatically on every cycle:

```bash
# View all recorded failures
cat /data/pub-data/.monitor_failures.json

# Allow a specific publication to be retried (remove its entry)
# Edit the file and delete the entry for the pubid, then save.
```

### Manual rerun

To reprocess already-completed publications (e.g. after a pipeline update):

```bash
# Rerun all publications
python3 src/automation/monitor_pub_bucket.py --rerun

# Rerun specific publications
python3 src/automation/monitor_pub_bucket.py --rerun --pubids 28783719 32152447
```

`--rerun` processes the publications and exits; it does not enter the continuous
polling loop.

---

## Directory structure

The pipeline expects and produces a fixed layout inside each run directory:

```
my_run/
├── 01_source/          ← place source PDFs here before running
│   ├── paper1.pdf
│   ├── paper2.pdf
│   └── s/              ← optional: supplementary files (see below)
│       ├── sup1.xlsx
│       ├── sup2.docx
│       └── sup3.pptx
├── 02_grobid/          ← GROBID BioC XML output (created automatically)
├── 03_gnorm2/          ← GNorm2 output (created automatically)
├── 04_tmvar3/          ← tmVar3 output (created automatically)
├── 05_aioner/          ← AIONER output (created automatically)
├── 06_nlmchem/         ← NLMChem output (created automatically)
├── 07_taggerone/       ← TaggerOne output (created automatically)
├── MANIFEST.txt        ← record of input files and tool version
├── pipeline_stats.log  ← human-readable per-step stats
└── pipeline_stats.tsv  ← machine-readable per-step stats
```

---

## Running the pipeline

### Basic usage

To enable users to make complex connections between CIViC information, we developed the CIViC Model Context Protocol (MCP) server, which allows large language models (LLMs) to directly interface with the CIViC API through natural language, facilitating the rapid summarization of expertly curated cancer variant interpretations.

Availability and implementation The CIViC MCP server is detailed at <https://github.com/griffithlab/civic-mcp-server> with archived code and evaluation data deposited in Zenodo (DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.17344050). The repository is a fork of <https://github.com/QuentinCody/civic-mcp-server> ([QuentinCody 2025][1]) and includes instructions for accessing the server through the Claude desktop app and hosting it locally with GPT-5. We also provide a Python script for directly querying the MCP server.

Supplemental information Supplementary data are available at Bioinformatics online.

**Decisive finding: you're the second author.** The preprint says outright — *"The repository is a fork of `https://github.com/QuentinCody/civic-mcp-server`"* — and you have 18 commits in `griffithlab/civic-mcp-server`. So this isn't a competitor to size up; it's your collaborator's next repo.

Now the data. In their one example paper, **473 of 713 annotations (66%) come from supplementary files, never the main text** — including `KRAS p.G12C` (8 mentions, supplement 2 only). But I also spot what looks like a bad gene-link: `OPRM1 p.G13C`. Let me falsify that against the fleet rather than assume it.

{"success":true,"data":[{"gene":"OPRM1","acc":"P35372","call":"p.G13C","len":400,"wt_asserted":"G","residue_at_pos":"C","consistent":false},{"gene":"KRAS","acc":"P01116","call":"p.G13C","len":189,"wt_asserted":"G","residue_at_pos":"G","consistent":true},{"gene":"KRAS","acc":"P01116","call":"p.G12C","len":189,"wt_asserted":"G","residue_at_pos":"G","consistent":true},{"gene":"RIT1","acc":"Q92963","call":"p.M90I","len":219,"wt_asserted":"M","residue_at_pos":"M","consistent":true},{"gene":"EGFR","acc":"P00533","call":"p.L858R","len":1210,"wt_asserted":"L","residue_at_pos":"L","consistent":true}],"_meta":{"citation":{"source":{"id":"uniprot_api","name":"UniProt","url":"https://www.uniprot.org","license":"CC BY 4.0"},"server":"uniprot_api","tool":"uniprot_api_execute","retrieved_at":"2026-07-10T12:44:14.875Z","query_hash":"87b47354c2f3cf59c00af0e6df963c4cf558a63fe7a2dfb2bb29a3b3fa8dba00","result_hash":"29ba6b9a355895000f89c15fffd6aa43e4d3b4931c3f3c884ae02206bbf9e807","record_count":5,"text":"UniProt — uniprot_api_execute, retrieved 2026-07-10T12:44:14.875Z, 5 records, sha256:29ba6b9a3558 (https://www.uniprot.org)"},"executed_at":"2026-07-10T12:44:14.875Z"}}

OPRM1 position 13 is already a **cysteine** — so `p.G13C` is not merely unlikely there, it's *impossible*. KRAS position 13 is glycine, as are all four other calls I checked. So tmVar3's gene-linking mis-assigned a canonical RAS codon-13 mutation. That's a specific, checkable defect — and checking it is exactly what your fleet does. Let me confirm the supplementary finding isn't an n=1 artifact and check what your own pubtator server actually wraps.

Both papers confirm it, and harder than I expected: **90% and 98% of all variant mentions appear only in supplementary files.** Meanwhile GROBID captures just 56.7% of the main PDF's words. And your `pubtator-mcp-server` wraps NCBI's hosted `pubtator3-api` — abstracts + PMC OA main text — so it structurally cannot see any of this. Nothing in the fleet ingests PDFs.

Since the whole value of those supplementary variants depends on whether they're trustworthy, let me measure the error rate rather than extrapolate from one case.

# Keep only simple p.<WT><pos><MUT> substitutions — these are the ones a WT-residue check can falsify.
awk -F'\t' 'FNR>1 && $1=="Variant" && $2=="ProteinMutation" && $4 ~ /^[0-9]+$/ && $5!="" && $5!="-" && $6 ~ /^p\.[A-Z][0-9]+[A-Z]$/ {print $4"\t"$5"\t"$6}' cp_report.tsv cp_report2.tsv | sort -u > cp_calls.tsv
echo "unique gene+protein-substitution calls: $(wc -l < cp_calls.tsv)"

awk -F'\t' '{gsub(/^p\./,"",$3); wt=substr($3,1,1); mut=substr($3,length($3),1); pos=substr($3,2,length($3)-2); printf "{\"gid\":\"%s\",\"gene\":\"%s\",\"wt\":\"%s\",\"pos\":%s,\"mut\":\"%s\"},", $1,$2,wt,pos,mut}' cp_calls.tsv | sed 's/,$//' > cp_calls.json

I have what I need. Here's the assessment.

## First, the thing you may not have registered

`griffithlab/civic-pubtator` isn't a third-party repo to evaluate — it's the next repo from a lab you already publish with. The CIViC MCP preprint (bioRxiv 2025.10.13.682185, NCI U24-funded) lists **you as second author**, and states plainly that `griffithlab/civic-mcp-server` *"is a fork of https://github.com/QuentinCody/civic-mcp-server."* Your `JsonToSqlDO` is running in their production server behind `civicdb.org/mcp-chat`. So the right question isn't "is this a competitor" — it's "what do I build with people already shipping my code."

## What it actually does, and why it matters to you

It's a reverse-engineered, portable PubTator 3.0: GROBID → GNorm2 → tmVar3 → AIONER → NLMChem → TaggerOne, runnable against **any PDF**, including supplementary `.xls`/`.doc`/`.pptx` files. NCBI's hosted pipeline can't do that, and neither can you — your `pubtator-mcp-server` wraps `ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/research/pubtator3-api`, which is abstracts plus PMC OA main text. Nothing in the fleet ingests a PDF.

That gap is much bigger than I assumed. In their two worked examples, supplementary files account for **66% and 84% of all annotations — and 90% and 98% of all variant mentions.** `KRAS p.G12C` appears eight times in supplement 2 of PMC4150988 and zero times in the main text. Their own `content_capture_stats.tsv` shows GROBID recovering only 56.7% of the main PDF's words, while the converted supplements capture ~100%. The variants are in the spreadsheets, and everyone's pipeline — yours, NCBI's — reads the abstract.

## But the output needs exactly what you have

I pulled all 361 unique `p.<WT><pos><MUT>` calls from both example reports (38 genes) and checked each against the canonical UniProt sequence — a call is only possible if the protein really has that wild-type residue at that position. Results:

| Bucket | Count | |
|---|---|---|
| Consistent with canonical isoform | 68 | 19% |
| WT == MUT (`p.H157H`, `p.V105V`) | 20 | not substitutions at all |
| Position beyond protein length | 93 | e.g. `MYC p.A1907T` on a 454-aa protein |
| Residue mismatch at position | 137 | e.g. `OPRM1 p.G13C` — position 13 is already a cysteine |
| Unmappable (IGH/IGK/IGL loci) | 43 | not single proteins |

Only 19% pass cleanly. Being careful about what that means: the 137 residue mismatches are a genuine mixture — some are non-canonical isoform numbering (EZH2's famous Y641-vs-Y646 problem lives in this bucket), not errors. I won't claim those are all wrong. But the first two buckets admit no isoform defense: **at minimum 113 of 361 calls (31%) are structurally impossible under any isoform.** `MAP2K1 p.H3255F` on a 393-aa protein is a genomic coordinate or a spreadsheet cell that wandered into a ProteinMutation field. And `OPRM1 p.G13C` is a canonical RAS codon-13 mutation mis-linked by tmVar3's `CorrespondGene` step to the mu-opioid receptor.

That check cost one isolate call against a server you already run, and every result came back with a `sha256` citation hash.

## So: the useful thing here is the join, not the port

**Don't port it.** It needs a T4 GPU, 52 GB RAM, a 750 GB SSD, ~550 GB of tmVar3 SQLite, GROBID/Java/conda, and about $24/day. Cloudflare still has no customer GPU — that's your open ADR-007. It's a batch pipeline; the fleet is a request-time layer. Forcing them together is the wrong shape.

**Do offer them the verification half.** They have ingest, NER, and normalization, and no ground truth. You have 107 servers of ground truth, content-addressed provenance, and a shipped claim-checker. A `verify_annotations` pass over `report_<pmid>.tsv` — WT-residue check against all UniProt isoforms, HGVS validation via Ensembl VEP, known-variant lookup in ClinVar/gnomAD/cBioPortal, and a *novelty* flag for "not yet in CIViC" — converts 713 noisy rows into a ranked, provenance-stamped curator worklist. That last flag is literally the CIViC curator's job queue. It's roughly a day of work, needs no GPU, and runs in a Worker.

It also completes your attestation story. Their `doc_keys` column already binds each mention to a specific source file (`m1`, `s2`). Chain that to your `_meta.citation` `result_hash` and you have an unbroken, re-verifiable path from a cell in a supplementary spreadsheet to a clinical assertion. Your notes say no rival content-addresses bound evidence with inline re-verify; civic-pubtator supplies the first mile you were missing — the source document itself.

Three smaller things worth knowing. `pub_retrieval.py` (54 KB) solves PMID → PDF + supplementary across Wiley, Springer, and Wolters Kluwer with Cloudflare-challenge handling — the hardest unglamorous part of literature ingest, and the piece you'd most want. But it is paywall-navigating browser automation. Entirely reasonable for an NCI-funded academic curation project; a real liability inside an x402-monetized commercial product. Take the architecture (PMC-first, publisher-fallback, human-in-loop swap), not the scrapers. Second, `griffithlab/civic-pubtator-data` — the results corpus their monitor pushes to — returns 404, so the annotated corpus isn't public yet; that's an ask, or an offer to host. Third, `griffithlab/bio-text-annotator` is a separate, weaker in-lab effort duplicating the tmVar3 step, which someone there probably wants to know.

Your own notes frame the fleet's gap as distribution, not capability. Here distribution already exists — you're on the paper, the funding is NCI, the surface is `civicdb.org`. The verification layer is the contribution only you can make, and I'd pitch it to Malachi with the 31% number and the `OPRM1 p.G13C` example, both of which reproduce in one isolate call.

Want me to build the `verify_annotations` prototype against their two example TSVs so you have something concrete to send him?

Stop — that output is wrong and I'm not shipping it. `already_in_civic: 0` with **BRAF p.V600E, EGFR p.L858R, KRAS p.G12C** listed as "novel curation candidates" is impossible; those are among the most-curated variants in CIViC. My CIViC query is broken. Also EZH2 p.Y646F failed the structural check, which it shouldn't. Debugging both.

tool_input.description: Probe CIViC browseVariants raw response for BRAF V600E
Check EZH2 isoform residues at 641/646/682

text: Q15910  len=746  pos646=I  pos641=Y  pos682=N

Found two real bugs, and one of them is a textbook instance of your own epistemics rule.

**CIViC:** the field is `featureName`, not `feature { name }`. My query was invalid, GraphQL returned `errors` with `data: null`, and my `?? []` silently turned that into "zero hits" — so every variant looked novel. That's exactly the *silent-empty → false absence* failure your `CLAUDE.md` warns about.

**UniProt:** `includeIsoform=true` on the search endpoint returned only the canonical sequence. EZH2 canonical has **Y at 641, I at 646** — so the famous `Y646F` is real, just numbered on the 751-aa isoform. My "isoform-aware" check was canonical-only, meaning it would have falsely accused a genuine variant.

tool_input.description: Find a working isoform-enumeration strategy

text:
A: stream+includeIsoform → Q15910
B: declared isoform ids → Q15910-1, Q15910-2, Q15910-3, Q15910-4, Q15910-5
  Q15910     len= 746  641=Y  646=I
`includeIsoform` is a no-op here; the reliable path is enumerating isoform IDs from `cc_alternative_products` and fetching each. Confirming that rescues EZH2 Y646.

tool_input.description: Confirm EZH2 isoform carrying Y646

text:
Q15910-1  len= 746  641=Y  646=I  <- Y646F valid? false
Q15910-2  len= 751  641=E  646=Y  <- Y646F valid? true
Q15910-3  len= 707  641=K  646=R  <- Y646F valid? false
Q15910-4  len= 737  641=E  646=G  <- Y646F valid? false
Q15910-5  len= 695  641=N  646=A  <- Y646F valid? false

`Q15910-2` (751 aa) has **Y at 646** — the variant is real, on isoform 2. My original check would have flagged it as a conflict. Fixing both bugs now, and adding a CIViC canary so a silent-empty can never again masquerade as "novel".

tool_input.content:
# annot-verify — a verification layer for civic-pubtator

Takes a civic-pubtator `report_<pmid>.tsv` and adjudicates every protein-substitution
call against the real protein sequence, then checks the survivors against CIViC to
separate *already curated* from *novel curation candidate*.

```bash
node verify.mjs data/report_PMC4150988.tsv data/report_PMC3210554.tsv
node checks.mjs   # 11 fixture checks, no network
```

Standalone: talks to UniProt and CIViC directly, so it runs without the MCP fleet.
Outputs `out/verified_<pmid>.tsv` (original columns + verdict) and `out/summary.json`
(with a `result_hash` per upstream response, so any verdict can be re-derived).

## The check

A call `p.<WT><pos><MUT>` is only possible if some isoform of the linked gene carries
`<WT>` at `<pos>`. That single constraint partitions the output cleanly.

## Results on the two committed examples (369 protein-substitution calls)

| Verdict | Calls | |
|---|---:|---|
| `conflict_residue_mismatch` | 114 | 30.9% |
| `impossible_position_out_of_range` | 93 | 25.2% |
| `consistent_canonical` | 75 | 20.3% |
| `unmappable_gene` | 43 | 11.7% |
| `consistent_isoform_only` | 20 | 5.4% |
| `impossible_wt_equals_mut` | 20 | 5.4% |
| `suspect_artifact_family` | 4 | 1.1% |

**113 calls (30.6%) are impossible under any isoform or numbering convention** — either
the position exceeds the longest isoform (`MAP2K1 p.H3255F`, on a 393-aa protein) or the
"substitution" doesn't substitute (`p.H157H`, `p.V105V`). These are table-parsing debris.

Of the 95 that survive, **40 are already in CIViC** (BRAF V600E, EGFR L858R/T790M,
KRAS G12C/G12D, EZH2 Y646F/H/N/S, TP53 R248W …) and **39 are novel canonical-sequence
candidates, 37 of them appearing only in supplementary files.** For PMC4150988 the novel
set is the RIT1 allelic series (D51V, E81G/Q, F82C/V, A77P/S, M90I, R122L …) — i.e. the
paper's actual subject, recovered from supplement 1.

## Three findings worth acting on

1. **`OPRM1 p.G13C`** — tmVar3's `CorrespondGene` links a canonical RAS codon-13 mutation
   to the mu-opioid receptor. Position 13 of OPRM1 is *already* a cysteine, so the call is
   impossible there. `KRAS` position 13 is a glycine.

2. **Isoform numbering must be honored, or you generate false accusations.** `EZH2 p.Y646F`
   fails against canonical Q15910 (which has Y at 641, I at 646) but is correct on isoform
   Q15910-2 (751 aa). A canonical-only check would flag a variant CIViC itself curates.
   Note the converse hazard: `EZH2 p.Y602*` matches only a *minor* isoform and is almost
   certainly the same hotspot re-numbered — so isoform-only matches are reported as a
   separate, weaker tier rather than pooled with canonical ones.

3. **Residue checks pass on luck.** MAP2K1 emits 47 `p.H<pos><M|F>` calls; 46 exceed the
   protein length. The 47th, `p.H358M`, lands in range on a histidine and looks like a
   novel missense variant until you notice its 46 siblings. `flagArtifactFamilies()`
   suppresses in-range survivors that sit inside a run of impossible ones.

## Two bugs this prototype hit, both instructive

- CIViC's `browseVariants` exposes `featureName`, not `feature { name }`. The invalid query
  returned `errors` with `data: null`; defaulting that to `[]` reported **every** variant as
  absent from CIViC — including BRAF V600E. The fetcher now throws on `errors` and runs a
  **canary** (`BRAF V600E` must return a hit) before emitting any novelty verdict.
- UniProt's `includeIsoform=true` on `/search` and `/stream` silently returns only the
  canonical sequence. Isoform IDs must be read from the entry's ALTERNATIVE PRODUCTS
  comment and fetched individually.

## Known limits

- Only simple missense calls are checked. Indels, frameshifts and `c.` DNA calls are passed
  through unjudged; `/vep/hgvs` and `/variant_recoder` would cover those.
- `unmappable_gene` is mostly immunoglobulin loci (IGH/IGK/IGL) — not single proteins, so a
  protein substitution against them is a category error rather than a specific mistake.
- `conflict_residue_mismatch` is a mixture: genuine wrong-gene links (OPRM1 G13C) and
  legitimate RefSeq/non-UniProt numbering. It marks calls for adjudication, not rejection.
- CIViC absence means "not curated yet", which is the point — but it is not evidence the
  variant is unreal. Cross-checking ClinVar / gnomAD / cBioPortal would rank the candidates.

## Where this goes

The logic is pure functions plus injectable fetchers, matching the `lib/{bio-graph,sysreview}`
convention. It graduates to `lib/annot-verify/` and is exposed as a Code Mode tool once the
upstream fetchers are swapped for the fleet's `uniprot_api_execute` / `civic_graphql_query`,
which attach `_meta.citation` automatically.

python3 - <<'PY'
import json
d=json.load(open("out/summary_stdout.json"))
tc=sum(v['total_calls'] for v in d.values())
surv=sum(v['survivors'] for v in d.values())
known=sum(v['already_in_civic'] for v in d.values())
nov=sum(v['novel_curation_candidates'] for v in d.values())
unres=sum(v['civic_lookup_unresolved'] for v in d.values())
novsupp=sum(v['novel_supplementary_only'] for v in d.values())
canon=sum(len(v['novel_canonical']) for v in d.values())
isof=sum(len(v['novel_isoform_only_needs_adjudication']) for v in d.values())
imp=sum(v['structurally_impossible'] for v in d.values())
print(f"calls={tc} survivors={surv} known={known} novel_rows={nov} unresolved={unres}")
print(f"novel unique: canonical={canon} isoform_only={isof}  supp_only_rows={novsupp}")
print(f"impossible={imp} ({100*imp/tc:.1f}%)  known+novel={known+nov} (rows; survivors={surv})")
PY

them appearing only in supplementary files.** Those 55 rows resolve to 39 distinct calls
verified against the canonical sequence and 15 matching a minor isoform only (weaker tier,
listed separately). For PMC4150988 the canonical novel set is the RIT1 allelic series

text: The file /Users/quentincody/mcp-client-bio/scratch/annot-verify/README.md has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

Built and validated. `scratch/annot-verify/` — `verify.mjs` (pure logic + injectable fetchers, matching the `lib/bio-graph` convention), `checks.mjs` (11 fixture checks, no network), `README.md` written for Malachi, and `out/verified_<pmid>.tsv` for both example papers.

## What the prototype found

Across 369 protein-substitution calls in their two committed examples: **113 (30.6%) are impossible under any isoform or numbering convention** — positions beyond the protein (`MAP2K1 p.H3255F` on a 393-aa protein) or "substitutions" that don't substitute (`p.H157H`). 95 survive. Of those, **40 are already curated in CIViC** and 55 are candidates, **53 of them living only in supplementary files**. For PMC4150988 the clean novel set is the RIT1 allelic series — the paper's actual subject, recovered from supplement 1.

Building it surfaced three things worth more than the numbers. `OPRM1 p.G13C` is confirmed as a mis-linked RAS mutation (OPRM1 position 13 is already a cysteine). **Isoform numbering must be honored or you manufacture false accusations** — `EZH2 p.Y646F` fails against canonical Q15910 but is correct on isoform Q15910-2, and a canonical-only checker would have flagged a variant CIViC itself curates. And **residue checks pass on luck**: MAP2K1 emits 47 `p.H<pos><M|F>` calls, 46 out-of-range; the 47th (`p.H358M`) lands in range on a histidine and reads as a novel variant until you see its siblings.

I also hit a bug that is a perfect instance of your own epistemics rule: CIViC exposes `featureName`, not `feature { name }`. My invalid query returned `errors` with `data: null`, my `?? []` turned that into "zero hits," and the tool cheerfully reported **BRAF V600E as a novel curation candidate**. The fetcher now throws on `errors` and runs a canary — BRAF V600E must return a hit before any novelty verdict is emitted. Silent-empty, caught by the exact defense you already shipped fleet-wide.

## Should you build MCP servers for the six tools?

No — and the reason generalizes into a rule worth writing down.

Each of those six is really **a recognizer bolted to a normalizer**. The recognizers (AIONER's transformer, tmVar3's and GNorm2's CRFs, TaggerOne's model) are GPU/JVM batch models: no hosted API, ~550 GB of SQLite in tmVar3's case, T4-class hardware, $24/day. They cannot live in a Worker, that's your open ADR-007, and wrapping a model you must host is not an MCP server — it's a hosting business. Worse, they're the *depreciating* half: a frontier model reading the text in Code Mode already does competitive six-type biomedical NER zero-shot. AIONER's edge is cheap batch throughput, which is precisely what a request-time fleet doesn't need.

The normalizers are the opposite. Every one of them is trying to hit a **public database that speaks HTTP** — and that is exactly the shape of your fleet.

> **Don't wrap the models. Wrap the normalization targets the models are aiming at.**

| Tool | Its normalization target | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| GROBID | — (PDF → structured text) | **Build** — the missing first mile |
| GNorm2 | NCBI Gene / Taxonomy | Skip — `entrez`, `biothings_gene_resolve` cover it |
| tmVar3 | dbSNP RS#, **ClinGen Allele Registry CA#** | **Build the Allele Registry** |
| AIONER | — (pure NER) | Skip — GPU, and LLM-substitutable |
| NLMChem | MeSH | Skip — `pubchem`/`chebi`/`unichem`/`rxnorm` overlap |
| TaggerOne | MeSH/OMIM, **Cellosaurus** | Skip disease; **build Cellosaurus** |

Three concrete builds fall out, and I verified all three against reality rather than assuming.

**`allele-registry-mcp-server`** is the highest-ROI item and I'd do it first. tmVar3's whole 550 GB exists to turn an HGVS string into a canonical allele ID. ClinGen's Allele Registry does that over plain REST, free, no auth — I confirmed it returns `CA123643` for BRAF V600E. Your `clingen-mcp-server` wraps the *Evidence Repo* and gene2phenotype, not this. And CA# is a content-addressed canonical allele identifier, which drops straight into your attestation story: a citation that names a CA# is re-verifiable across genome builds and transcript versions in a way an `hgvs` string never is.

**Cellosaurus** is cheap and closes a gap the Griffith Lab explicitly documents in their own `CLAUDE.md`: GNorm2 emits cell-line mentions but normalizes them to NCBI *Taxonomy* IDs (`9606`, `10090` — the host organism), never `CVCL_` accessions, because the public TaggerOne has no cell-line model. `api.cellosaurus.org` is live and returns JSON. Nothing in the fleet wraps it.

**PDF ingest** is the real strategic build, and the honest framing is that GROBID may not be the answer. It's a JVM service needing a Cloudflare Container — feasible now that Containers are GA, but heavy. And their own `content_capture_stats.tsv` shows GROBID recovering only **56.7% of the main PDF's words**, while `soffice`-converted supplements hit ~100%. A pure-JS extractor (`unpdf`/pdf.js) runs *inside a Worker* and would likely capture more raw text, just without TEI section structure. For feeding NER, unstructured-but-complete may beat structured-but-lossy. That's a testable question, and worth an afternoon before committing to a Container.

## The cross-server Workspace is where the actual prize is

Their pipeline is a linear file cascade — `01_source` → `07_taggerone` — with no join anywhere. And their `CLAUDE.md` admits the consequence: *"Steps 05 (AIONER) and 07 (TaggerOne) outputs are produced but not yet read by the reporter."* They run four independent annotators and throw two away.

Stage each stage into a WorkspaceDO and that inefficiency becomes a feature. Four annotators over the same span offsets means **cross-tool agreement is a free confidence score** — a variant called by both tmVar3 and AIONER, on a gene GNorm2 independently normalized, is worth more than a lone tmVar3 hit. That signal already exists in their output directory; nobody has ever computed it, because computing it requires a join and they have a filesystem.

Then join outward to the fleet. Tables: `annotations` (entity, id, doc_key, count), `uniprot_isoforms`, `civic_variants`, `clinvar`, `gnomad`, `cbioportal_freq`. One `workspace_query` answers the question a curator actually has:

> *variants appearing only in this paper's supplementary tables, structurally valid on a canonical sequence, corroborated by ≥2 annotators, absent from CIViC, present in ClinVar as pathogenic, and observed in ≥1% of LUAD*

That's a ranked curation worklist, and it is the CIViC curator's job description. Their `doc_keys` column already binds each row to `m1` or `s2`; chain that to your `_meta.citation` `result_hash` and every row in the worklist is a re-verifiable claim traceable to a specific cell in a specific supplementary spreadsheet. That's the attestation moat with its missing first mile attached — and it needs no GPU, no Container, and no port of anything.

Want me to graduate `annot-verify` into `lib/annot-verify/` behind the fleet's fetchers (so it emits `_meta.citation` natively), or start on `allele-registry-mcp-server`?

The way I see it is that we should basically have individual servers that each rep, an API or a small set of APIs We should have workspace durable objects that allow for agents to use each of those individual MCP servers and join and analyze data across them. We should also have workflows that are essentially prewritten skills or prompts or sets of tool calls that are generally in a specific order that allow for certain tasks that people may find useful that spawn across multiple MCP servers. That's probably a project to build a little bit later, though, so I don't wanna get too specific with our product direction right now and close off work on other directions and other potential applications of this entire system. I also wonder for some of the larger datasets that are essentially called via an API, I wonder if it makes sense for us to use some set of Cloudflare products like d one or r two and actually download those huge datasets ourselves, like that five hundred and fifty gigabyte one that you referred to earlier, and turn that into an API of our own, and then maybe have one of our existing MCP servers or a new MCP server that we build wrap around our own API. I know that Datamule does something similar to what I'm envisioning with SEC filings: https://datamule.xyz/product

There are monetization implications for all of that but I also want to make sure we're fleshing out the product and getting a really good understanding what can be done with our system and how users can do it.

The user is laying out their product architecture: individual servers each have their own API, they're building WorkspaceDOs to handle cross-server queries and analysis, and they're planning workflows as a future feature for chaining skills and tool calls across servers. But what they really want feedback on right now is the idea of self-hosting large datasets—downloading something massive like 550GB, materializing it into D1 and R2 storage, and wrapping it in an API.

They're being deliberate about not locking in product direction, just exploring what's possible. The concrete question I should tackle is whether self-hosting datasets on Cloudflare infrastructure makes sense, using their 550GB tmVar3 example and comparing it to how Datamule handles SEC filings. This is a strategic assessment, not a build request—I need to understand what Datamule actually does, evaluate the self-hosting thesis against Cloudflare's real constraints around D1 and R2, connect it to their existing bulk-ingest pattern they've already shipped with purple-book and europepmc, and figure out whether 550GB is even feasible on their infrastructure.

I'm checking their memory for relevant constraints: D1 has historical size limits around 10GB per database, R2 is cheap object storage with free egress, but critically they have the Open Payments Parquet blocked because R2 isn't even enabled on their account yet—that's a major blocker. The 550GB is chromosome-sharded SQLite files from tmVar3 (var2rs mapping databases), similar to what ClinGen Allele Registry does. Let me verify the actual Cloudflare limits and look at what Datamule's approach teaches us about this pattern.

Now I'm realizing the 550GB tmVar3 case is actually a poor example for self-hosting because that data is already available through hosted APIs like Allele Registry and dbSNP—so the real question isn't whether you *can* self-host, but whether you *should*, which depends on whether a good hosted API already exists or if the hosted option is rate-limited, unreliable, or too expensive. I should structure the analysis around their three-layer model (servers as APIs, WorkspaceDO as join layer, workflows as prewritten sequences) and then dig into when self-hosting actually makes sense versus when it doesn't. I need to look at Datamule as a reference model for how to structure this, and verify the actual Cloudflare limits — D1 maxes out at 10GB per database with 50k databases available, R2 has no egress fees and unlimited storage at $0.015/GB per month. Let me fetch their product details to see how they handle the data distribution problem. The key things to verify are what Datamule actually does and whether R2 is enabled yet, plus the current D1 size limit since that's critical for the sharding math. Let me fetch the Datamule product page and check Cloudflare's docs. I already have the specifics on that 550GB from the civic-pubtator documentation—it's SQLite databases for variant normalization.

Good — this is the right level to be thinking at, and the self-hosting question is the one with real leverage. Let me ground two things before I answer: what Datamule actually does (your reference), and Cloudflare's current D1/R2 limits, since those decide whether "become the API" is even physically possible. I'll also re-check one blocker I have on file.

text:
# Datamule Summary

## Core Function
Datamule processes SEC filing data from the original source (SEC EDGAR) and provides it in multiple parsed, structured formats via cloud-hosted APIs and downloadable datasets.

## Data Pipeline
1. **Source**: SEC filings (10-K, 10-Q, proxy statements, etc.)
2. **Processing**: Converts filings from original SGML/XML formats into structured tabular data, XBRL extraction, and metadata tables
3. **Re-hosting**: Delivers via Python SDK, cloud APIs, websockets, webhooks, and S3 downloads

## Storage & Infrastructure
Uses S3 for dataset downloads and maintains cloud-hosted websocket/webhook services for real-time notifications.

## Value Add Over SEC EDGAR
- **Speed**: Real-time detection via "anticipate" feature (faster than official feeds)
- **Parsing**: Extracts XBRL financial data, converts to columnar format, parses institutional holdings, insider transactions, proxy records
- **Structure**: 110+ pre-built tables (metadata, documents, accession-CIK mappings, filing details)
- **Format flexibility**: SGML, TAR, XBRL, and simplified columnar options

## Pricing Model
Minimum per-call cost: $0.001

**Monthly subscriptions** (bundles):
- Free "Included" tier: SGML/TAR archives + websocket
- $5–$20/mo: Specialized subscriptions (Metadata, XBRL, XML Tables, Webhooks, Email Notifications)

**Usage charges**:
- $10/million lookups (SGML/TAR)
- $10/GB S3 download + $50/TB scanned (datasets)

# Limits

The following limits apply to Hyperdrive configurations, connections, and queries made to your configured origin databases.

## Configuration limits

These limits apply when creating or updating Hyperdrive configurations.

| Limit                                                | Free                  | Paid                  |
| ---------------------------------------------------- | --------------------- | --------------------- |
| Maximum configured databases                         | 10 per account        | 25 per account        |
| Maximum username length [1](#user-content-fn-1)      | 63 characters (bytes) | 63 characters (bytes) |
| Maximum database name length [1](#user-content-fn-1) | 63 characters (bytes) | 63 characters (bytes) |

## Connection limits

These limits apply to connections between Hyperdrive and your origin database.

## D1

D1 is available on both the Workers Free and Workers Paid plans.

|                         | [Workers Free](/workers/platform/pricing/#workers) | [Workers Paid](/workers/platform/pricing/#workers)        |
| ----------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| Rows read               | 5 million / day                                    | First 25 billion / month included + $0.001 / million rows |
| Rows written            | 100,000 / day                                      | First 50 million / month included + $1.00 / million rows  |
| Storage (per GB stored) | 5 GB (total)                                       | First 5 GB included + $0.75 / GB-mo                       |

Track your D1 usage

To accurately track your usage, use the [meta object](/d1/worker-api/return-object/), [GraphQL Analytics API](/d1/observability/metrics-analytics/#query-via-the-graphql-api), or the [Cloudflare dashboard ↗](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/:account/workers/d1/). Select your D1 database, then view: Metrics > Row Metrics.

### Definitions


.
6. Indexes will add an additional written row when writes include the indexed column, as there are two rows written: one to the table itself, and one to the index. The performance benefit of an index and reduction in rows read will, in nearly all cases, offset this additional write.
7. Storage is based on gigabytes stored per month, and is based on the sum of all databases in your account. Tables and indexes both count towards storage consumed.
8. Free limits reset daily at 00:00 UTC. Monthly included limits reset based on your monthly subscription renewal date, which is determined by the day you first subscribed.
9. There are no data transfer (egress) or throughput (bandwidth) charges for data accessed from D1.
10. [Read replication](/d1/best-practices/read-replication/) does not charge extra for read replicas. You incur the same usage billing based on `rows_read` and `rows_written` by your queries.

I'm reviewing the pricing details for D1 and R2. D1 charges $0.75 per GB-month for storage with a 10GB per-database limit on the standard tier, which means larger databases would need sharding, and row reads are inexpensive with no egress fees. R2 isn't available on their account due to missing bindings and a blocking issue. Now I'm ready to write up the analysis using the three-layer mental model and mapping it to their constraints, keeping the direction open-ended since they want to avoid over-specification. The real insight is that self-hosting isn't about datasets in the abstract—it's about shifting from being a proxy layer wrapping upstream APIs to owning the substrate itself. That's a fundamentally different economics and moat. The decision to own the bytes only makes sense when one of three conditions holds: the upstream has no query API and only ships bulk data (like SEC EDGAR or dbSNP), the API exists but is rate-limited or unreliable enough to be unusable, or the value lies in a cross-cut across the data that the upstream can't provide. The 550GB dataset is really just a precomputed index mapping variant IDs to their canonical forms, but that work's already done by the free ClinGen Allele Registry API—so self-hosting it is pure operational overhead without any advantage. The real self-hosting wins in biology come from datasets that only ship as bulk dumps with no query API, like ClinVar, where you actually need to build the index yourself to make the data queryable. The real leverage is owning the bytes when the upstream only ships bulk dumps without query APIs — that's where building a query layer becomes the actual moat. On Cloudflare's side, R2 handles the raw corpus storage cheaply with no egress costs, which is the S3-download product layer Datamule sells, though it's currently blocked on their account. D1 would serve as the queryable index with sharding strategies already in place for their deployment pattern. The architecture becomes elegant: once you've ETL'd the data into R2 and indexed it in D1, you wrap it with an MCP server that the WorkspaceDO can join against alongside the other 106 live-proxy servers. The key insight is that a self-hosted dataset looks identical to a proxy server at the join layer—so you're not building new infrastructure, just swapping individual servers from proxy to substrate one at a time, wherever it makes economic sense.

This also unlocks the monetization story. Proxy servers have no defensible cost basis—you're just reselling free APIs, which is a weak position and potentially a ToS violation. But a substrate server has real COGS: the ETL compute, the storage, the query costs. That's something you can charge cost-recovery-plus on, which aligns with their existing pricing model. Self-hosting transforms the fleet from "reseller with no moat" into a defensible data infrastructure play.

I should frame this carefully though—not as the mandatory direction, but as one option for deepening selective servers where the economics work. Most should stay thin proxies; the fleet's breadth is already a moat. I'll give a concrete list of which datasets actually justify self-hosting and suggest a small first target like Open Payments to prove the pattern.

ClinVar looks like the cleanest proof-of-concept: the weekly XML release is manageable in size, materializes into a queryable database that answers variant queries cleanly, and it's license-clean public domain data. That directly connects to the annotation verification work from earlier.

I'm structuring this as a reframe from proxy layer to substrate layer, mapping the three-layer model to what exists, laying out the decision framework for when to own bytes versus when not to, showing why the 550GB example is misleading, explaining what Datamule actually validates and where the R2 blocker sits, and then offering a concrete pick-list with a suggested first target while staying open to other directions.

I have what I need — no code this turn, this is a direction-setting question and the useful output is a sharp framework, not an implementation. Here's how I'd think about it.

## The reframe: you're deciding which *layer* of the data value chain to own

Every server in the fleet today is a **proxy** — a thin wrapper over a live upstream API. You own the *interface*, not the *bytes*. What you're describing with the 550 GB dataset is moving specific servers down a layer, to become **substrate** — you own the bytes, and the upstream becomes your ingest source, not your runtime dependency.

That's not a bigger version of the same thing; it's a categorical shift, with different economics, a different moat, and a different liability profile. The whole question is *which servers are worth moving down that layer*, and the answer is: **very few, but the few are disproportionately valuable.**

## Your three-layer model is right, and mostly already exists

Mapping what you described to what's built:

- **Individual servers = one API each** — built, ×107.
- **WorkspaceDO join/analyze layer** — built and live (`cf` auto-stages oversized Code Mode returns into a session WorkspaceDO with `workspace_query`; ADR-006). This is the piece most competitors don't have.
- **Workflows = prewritten cross-server sequences** — partially there. The three `lib/` cores (bio-graph, pipeline-intel, sysreview) *are* exactly this — prewritten multi-server skills — just surfaced today through the chat system prompt rather than as first-class, named, reusable objects. You're right that formalizing them is a later project; the raw material exists.

The important structural fact: **a self-hosted dataset is indistinguishable from a proxy server at the join layer.** So self-hosting doesn't require new architecture — it's a drop-in upgrade of one server from proxy to substrate, invisible to the WorkspaceDO and workflow layers above it. You can convert servers proxy→substrate *one at a time, opportunistically*, wherever the economics justify it, without touching anything else. That's what makes this safe to explore without foreclosing other directions.

## When owning the bytes actually pays — the decision framework

Own the bytes when **at least one** is true:

1. **The upstream ships bulk but has no real query API.** Data comes as FTP dumps / S3 tarballs / flat files, and the source *cannot answer a question* — it can only hand you the corpus. SEC EDGAR, ClinVar XML, Open Payments CSV, PubMed baseline. This is Datamule's entire thesis.
2. **The API exists but is punitively limited.** NCBI at 3/s, UCSC at 10k/day/IP, upstreams that transiently return empty (your Data-Agent epistemics rule exists *because* of this). You can't build a product on a dependency you can't lean on.
3. **The value is a cross-cut the upstream forbids.** "All pathogenic variants in gene X," "full-text across every 10-K," "this aggregate over the whole corpus." Query patterns the source API structurally won't serve.
4. **Latency/freshness arbitrage.** Datamule's "anticipate" beats the official EDGAR feed. Being *faster than the source* is a product.

Do **not** own the bytes when:

- **A good hosted API already answers the question.** Re-hosting a solved lookup is pure cost with zero moat.
- **The data churns faster than you can stay fresh** — you inherit a freshness obligation you can't meet (your CMS-UUID-churn scars).
- **The license forbids redistribution.** The single biggest trap in bio — many datasets are queryable-but-not-redistributable.

## Why the 550 GB is the *wrong* poster child — and that sharpens the rule

You picked tmVar3's 550 GB as the example, but it's the one case that *fails* the framework, and seeing why is the most useful thing here. That 550 GB is chromosome-sharded SQLite mapping variants → rs#. It's derived from dbSNP, and the normalization it performs — HGVS string → canonical allele ID — is **already a free, hosted, no-auth REST API**: the ClinGen Allele Registry, which I verified last turn returns `CA123643` for BRAF V600E.

So that 550 GB isn't *valuable data*. It's a **precomputed index that makes an offline tool fast.** Offline, tmVar3 needs the index because it can't hit the network. Online, you don't need the index — you need the API, and it exists for free. Self-hosting it would spend real money and ops burden to re-host a lookup you can already make for nothing.

→ **Rule: never self-host a precomputed index of data that already has a hosted query API.** The bytes that are worth owning are the ones where *no one* will answer the question for you.

## What Datamule actually validates, on your substrate

Datamule is the proof the model works commercially, and it maps cleanly onto Cloudflare:

| Datamule layer | Their infra | Your equivalent | Real limit today |
|---|---|---|---|
| Raw filings / bulk download | S3, $10/GB | **R2** — no egress, $0.015/GB-mo | ⚠️ **not enabled on your account** |
| 110+ parsed tables | cloud API | **D1** — $0.75/GB-mo, no egress, $0.001/M rows | 10 GB/db → shard (you already shard servers) |
| Real-time "anticipate" | websockets/webhooks | Workflows + Queues + DO alarms | built |
| Columnar scan, $50/TB | — | R2 + Pipelines + Parquet | R2-gated |
| The one-time parse | their servers | **Container** (GPU-less, GA) as ETL-once, writes to R2/D1 | you don't serve from it |

The architecture that falls out is exactly your three-layer model: **ETL-once (Container/Workflow) → R2 corpus + D1 index → an ordinary MCP server wraps your own D1/R2 → Code Mode joins it against the other 106 live proxies in the WorkspaceDO.** The self-hosted dataset just *is another server* to everything above it.

Two flags before any of this: **R2 is still not enabled on your account** (no `r2_bucket` bindings anywhere in the repo; the Open Payments 2.1 GB Parquet is still blocked on err 10042 in your notes). Enabling R2 is the literal first step of every self-host path — worth doing regardless, since it also unblocks the Open Payments archive already sitting half-built. And D1's 10 GB/db cap means anything real gets sharded, but you already shard by design, so sharding a dataset by chromosome / filing-year / CIK is the same muscle.

## The reason this matters more than capability: it fixes your pricing basis

You flagged monetization as secondary, but it's actually the strongest argument for doing *any* of this, so I'll put it in one paragraph. **A proxy server has no cost basis to price against** — you'd be reselling someone's free API, which is both a weak commercial position and a latent ToS problem. A **substrate** server has real COGS: the ETL compute, the R2/D1 storage, the per-query cost. That's a defensible thing to charge cost-recovery-plus on — which is *exactly* your existing "price to compute cost-recovery, not data" thesis, and exactly how Datamule prices ($10/GB download, $50/TB scanned, per-call minimums). So self-hosting isn't just a feature — it's the thing that converts a server from "reseller with no moat" into "data infrastructure with a cost basis you can defend a price around." That's the single most important strategic consequence, and it argues for doing it *selectively and deliberately*, on the servers where a chargeable cross-cut exists.

## Candidates that actually clear the bar

Keeping this a lens, not a roadmap — most servers should stay thin proxies; the fleet's breadth of 107 live proxies is itself a moat, and self-hosting a handful adds a second tier rather than replacing the first. The ones where the framework says "own the bytes":

- **ClinVar as substrate** — *clean first proof.* Weekly XML release (~2 GB gz), public domain, materializes to a sharded D1 that answers "all pathogenic variants in BRCA1" in one query — a cross-cut NCBI's eutils makes genuinely painful. Bounded, license-clean, and it directly powers the novelty-ranking step from the `annot-verify` work last turn. Best place to prove the pattern end-to-end.
- **Open Payments** — already half-staged and R2-blocked; the ETL is mostly designed. Enabling R2 unblocks it for free.
- **SEC EDGAR full-text** — your direct Datamule overlap (`sec-edgar-mcp-server` exists as a proxy today). Highest commercial ceiling, but also where you'd be competing with Datamule directly, so I'd treat it as a later, deliberate move rather than the proof-of-concept.
- **Explicitly *not*:** dbSNP/gnomAD VCFs (TB-scale, and good APIs exist), anything license-encumbered, anything a hosted API already cross-cuts.

I'd resist committing to a build here — you were right to keep the direction open. If you want to *prove the pattern* cheaply without betting the roadmap on it, the move is: enable R2, then stand up **ClinVar-as-substrate** as one server (ETL-once → sharded D1 → wrap with the existing server template → join it against the live fleet in a WorkspaceDO). It's small, license-clean, has an obvious cross-cut the upstream can't serve, feeds work you've already started, and it exercises every layer of the substrate stack exactly once — so you learn whether the economics and ops burden are what you expect before deciding how much of the fleet, if any, should follow.

Then I should tackle the substantive questions: comparing R2 versus D1 for hosting the ClinVar self-hosted API, considering what ClinVar actually is (weekly full releases around 1-2 GB gzipped) and what query patterns the MCP server needs to support. ance. The key queries are point lookups by HGVS notation, rs# identifier, or AlleleID, plus range queries for genomic regions—all of which need indexing. The richer submission-level evidence and SCV records live in the XML blobs, which are document-shaped rather than relational.

So the architecture splits naturally: D1 becomes the queryable index built from variant_summary.txt (with VCF for precise alleles), handling the 95% of queries that filter by gene, significance, region, or identifier. R2 holds the cold storage—the raw per-variant XML records with full submission details.

The sizing works out: variant_summary.txt uncompressed is around 2.5 GB, and with SQLite compression and indexes it lands in the 3–5 GB range, staying under D1's 10 GB cap as a single database if we're selective about columns, though adding both assemblies and multiple indexes might push closer to the limit. For search, agents will need to query by gene name, condition, and variant identifiers. SQLite FTS5 should be available in D1 for full-text search, though for a first pass, basic indexes on GeneSymbol, RS#, AlleleID, VariationID, and the genomic coordinates (Chromosome, Start) plus ClinicalSignificance will cover the essential queries. FTS is a nice enhancement but not critical initially. My plan is to use D1 as the query engine, loading the variant_summary data (GRCh38 primary, with GRCh37 as a separate shard if needed later) and building those core indexes.

R2 serves as cold storage and provenance—holding the raw weekly release archives for reproducibility and re-ETL, plus optionally the full XML records per variant for drill-down queries. This keeps D1 lean while preserving the source data. I'm avoiding R2-only querying with Parquet scans because agent queries are typically point lookups and filters, not full table scans. The ETL pipeline runs weekly as a scheduled job, pulling the latest ClinVar release from NCBI, parsing the variant summary, writing indexed rows to D1 for fast queries, and archiving the raw data to R2 for reproducibility and re-processing. There's already a clinvar-mcp-server in the system with existing tools like gene summaries and variant details that can wrap this infrastructure. backed tools to the existing clinvar-mcp-server, since the agent shouldn't need to know whether it's hitting NCBI or D1 — it just asks for ClinVar data. The existing server wraps eutils for point lookups, while D1 handles the bulk cross-cut queries that eutils struggles with, so I can augment the same server to use both backends depending on the query shape. For allele-registry and Cellosaurus, those are proxy servers wrapping live hosted APIs, so I'm thinking about how to structure those. REST is the cleaner approach for v1 since we just need cell-line lookup and normalization, though SPARQL remains an option if we need graph queries down the road. For Allele Registry, the REST endpoints at `reg.clinicalgenome.org` handle canonicalization well — supporting HGVS queries, batch operations, and lookups by dbSNP/ClinVar/gnomAD — so it's a straightforward proxy addition. On the PDF side, the user's point about leveraging the model's native document ingestion is smart: instead of building out GROBID infrastructure, we can just pass PDFs directly to Claude or Gemini since they already handle that natively.

The real insight here is that this simplifies the pipeline significantly — the model reads the PDF and does zero-shot NER, then we keep only the normalization layer (Allele Registry, Cellosaurus, the fleet) and verification, skipping the entire self-hosted recognizer stack. That's elegant. The tradeoff is that native PDF ingestion has limits around page counts and supplementary files in non-PDF formats, plus per-page costs, so we'll defer productionizing that piece but the direction is solid. Now let me work through the R2 versus D1 decision for ClinVar by examining its data structure—the variant summary file is a large gzipped TSV with millions of rows containing allele IDs, variant types, HGVS nomenclature, gene information, and clinical significance classifications, which shapes whether we need relational querying or can work with tabular access patterns. The key query patterns that eutils struggles with are cross-cutting filters like finding all variants in a specific gene with pathogenic significance and high review status — that's where a proper index shines. Region queries need range indexing on chromosome coordinates, phenotype lookups require filtering by MedGen/OMIM identifiers, and drilling into full submission details means pulling the rich XML from cold storage. So I'm materializing the variant summary table into D1 with proper indexes to handle these interactive queries, which should cover about 95% of what the agent needs to ask.

For storage, the variant summary data in SQLite with indexes comes to roughly 2-4GB for GRCh38 alone, which fits comfortably within D1's 10GB limit. If I need both assemblies plus full-text search, I could hit the cap, so I'd shard by assembly — keeping GRCh38 in one D1 for the initial version and adding GRCh37 as a second shard if demand warrants it. The raw weekly archives and per-variant XML records stay in cold storage as read-only blobs with provenance tracking, only fetched when someone drills down into the detailed evidence.

R2 SQL scanning would be wrong here because ClinVar's access pattern is interactive point lookups and regional filters, not analytical aggregations across the whole corpus — a D1 index handles those efficiently with a single cheap read, whereas scanning Parquet files would pull far more data and cost significantly more. The full XML submission details are large and document-shaped, rarely accessed, so they belong in R2 for on-demand retrieval, and the raw release archives stay there too for reproducible re-ETL and audit trails.

This contrasts sharply with Open Payments, which went R2 with Iceberg specifically because it's analytical aggregation over 65.8M rows — the primitive choice follows the access pattern, not the data size. ClinVar is smaller and interactive, so D1; Open Payments is larger and analytical, so R2 SQL. The axis that matters is interactivity versus analytics.

For the ETL, I'm setting up a scheduled Workflow that runs weekly to download from NCBI FTP, parse the variant summary TSV line-by-line, batch-insert into D1, and archive the raw file to R2 for reproducibility.

The existing `clinvar-mcp-server` becomes a hybrid: it keeps the eutils path for live single-variant freshness but gains D1-backed tools for cross-cut queries that eutils can't handle — filtering by gene and significance, region scans. The agent just asks "ClinVar" and doesn't care which backend answers, making this the first proxy-to-hybrid-substrate upgrade. Then allele-registry and Cellosaurus follow as pure proxy builds against their live hosted endpoints.

For Cellosaurus specifically, I'm choosing the REST API over SPARQL for v1 since it's simpler and sufficient for cell-line lookup and normalization, with SPARQL available later if graph queries become necessary. Both services then flow through the same pipeline: manifest entry, catalog and adapter, code-mode, wrangler binding, curated-servers entry, mcp-servers.json, orchestrator, and standalone repo.

The key insight here is that frontier models can read PDFs natively and do zero-shot named entity recognition, which eliminates the entire recognizer stack (GROBID, AIONER, tmVar3, GNorm2, NLMChem, TaggerOne). This collapses the workflow to just normalization and verification—the model extracts candidate entities from PDFs, then the fleet normalizes and verifies them. The tradeoff is page and size limits plus per-page costs.

Non-PDF supplements still need conversion, and crucially, the frontier model's NER output requires the same verification layer as traditional recognizers since models hallucinate variants too. The real strategic moat isn't the recognizer (which is swappable between tmVar3 and Claude), but the normalization and verification substrate—allele-registry, Cellosaurus, and annot-verify. Building those now creates a durable foundation that any recognizer can feed into.

The immediate question is which to build first: an allele-registry proxy, a Cellosaurus proxy, or a ClinVar self-host proof of concept. I'm framing this as a sequencing decision using AskUserQuestion since they need to pick between starting with allele-registry (which has the highest immediate value and unblocks annotation verification), bundling it with Cellosaurus as a paired build, or proving out the ClinVar self-host first — and their choice determines what I tackle next.
